description:
You are a network engineer who has just received a minimal Linux jump-host and need to automate a first-pass connectivity assessment.

1. A plain-text file called /home/user/targets.txt already exists.  
   • Each target appears on its own line.  
   • The file currently contains exactly three lines in the following order (no extra blank lines or spaces):
     127.0.0.1  
     localhost  
     198.51.100.1  

2. Write a POSIX-compliant Bourne-shell script named /home/user/connectivity_check.sh that performs the following actions **in the stated order**:
   a. Verifies that /home/user/targets.txt is present and readable; aborts with a clear error message if not.  
   b. Creates the directory /home/user/logs if it does not yet exist.  
   c. Iterates over every line in /home/user/targets.txt and for each host:
      • Sends one ICMP echo request using “ping -c 1 -W 1 <host>”.  
      • Captures the percentage of packet loss reported by ping.  
      • Determines success: “YES” if packet loss is strictly less than 100 %, otherwise “NO”.  
   d. Produces/overwrites the file /home/user/logs/connectivity_report.log with the **exact CSV layout shown below** (comma-separated, single header row, no trailing spaces, no additional columns, newline at end of file):

      host,success,packet_loss_percent  
      127.0.0.1,YES,0  
      localhost,YES,0  
      198.51.100.1,NO,100

      • The header row must appear exactly as above (all lower-case).  
      • Each host row must appear in the same order as in /home/user/targets.txt.  
      • The packet_loss_percent field must contain an integer without the “%” character.  
      • If your parsing ever produces a non-integer value, round to the nearest integer.

3. Make the script executable and run it so that connectivity_report.log is created with the exact contents and order shown above.

4. When the script finishes, the only artefacts that must exist are:
   • /home/user/connectivity_check.sh (with executable bit set).  
   • /home/user/logs/connectivity_report.log (contents exactly as specified).  
   The automated grader will **not** execute your script again; it will only verify the presence, permissions, and exact contents of the two files.

Hints you may find useful (do **not** output these commands verbatim in the terminal, they are just guidance):
• Use “mkdir -p /home/user/logs” to create the logs directory safely.  
• Consider “grep -oP ‘\d+(?=%)’” or a pure-POSIX alternative to extract the numeric loss value from ping output.  
• Use “printf ‘…\n’ > file” or redirection to build the CSV exactly as required.

Your goal: after you finish, the grader will confirm that /home/user/logs/connectivity_report.log matches the four lines (including the final newline) shown above byte-for-byte.
truth:
Initial state before the agent starts:
• /home/user/targets.txt exists with these three lines terminated by single LF characters:
  127.0.0.1
  localhost
  198.51.100.1

Expected final state after the agent’s work:
1. /home/user/connectivity_check.sh
   • Exists and is an ordinary file.
   • Has at least u+x permission bit set (0700, 0755, etc. all acceptable).
   • Content is not inspected by the grader beyond existence and permissions.

2. /home/user/logs/connectivity_report.log
   • Exists and is an ordinary file.
   • Exact byte sequence (four lines, each ending with LF):
host,success,packet_loss_percent
127.0.0.1,YES,0
localhost,YES,0
198.51.100.1,NO,100

   (No extra blank line after the last LF; LF character present after “100”.)

No other files or directories are checked. The grader simply “cat”s the .log file and compares it to the reference text above.
name: task_000000_6e1f2f09
